input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin is a tele‑operated 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle that has been retrofitted with advanced sensors and control technology. Its hardware hierarchy comprises a lower‑level chassis, an upper‑level chassis, and dedicated autonomy elements (e.g., perception and actuation modules). The vehicle operates in the automotive Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) domain, specifically to demonstrate Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC). Its limited on‑board ECU resources make it a prime candidate for computational off‑loading, and its modular chassis design facilitates observable physical elements needed for digital twinning.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three core services: (1) **Data Monitoring** – a user interface (UI) presents real‑time vehicle state, projected driving path, and detected object tracks, and allows users to view, save, and generate reports of the data; (2) **Computation Off‑loading** – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, desired‑speed computation) is executed on the DT in the cloud, with the resulting speed command sent back to the vehicle for actuation; (3) **Remote Management** – the UI lets operators enable/disable ACC, set a desired speed, and issue an emergency‑brake command. The services are targeted at two user groups: operators (who monitor and manage the ACC remotely) and developers (who use the monitoring and data‑collection capabilities for troubleshooting). All services require low end‑to‑end latency communication, data collection, and reporting capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation uses the open‑source Automotive Digital Twin platform maintained by the McMaster Centre for Software Certification (McSCert) (GitHub repository: https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin) and demonstrated in a public video (https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU). The system architecture follows the ISO 23247 entity‑based reference model: the physical vehicle is the Observable Manufacturing Element, a Data Collection and Device Control Entity gathers sensor data and sends control commands, a Digital Twin Entity hosts the off‑loaded ACC computation and maintains the digital representation, and a User Entity provides the UI. Functional Entities within each main entity perform tasks such as data transformation and actuation. The documentation notes that OPC Unified Architecture could be used for data exchange, though the specific protocol employed is not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires clock synchronization between vehicle and digital twin with an accuracy of 100 ms, as mandated by safety‑critical vehicular standards and limited by the available hardware. Real‑time ACC services (data monitoring, computation off‑loading, remote management) demand low end‑to‑end latency communication; the off‑loaded ACC controller must receive sensor updates and return the desired speed within a latency budget comparable to the 100 ms synchronization window to maintain safe vehicle operation.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: A single Digital Twin System is composed of three hierarchical entities as defined by ISO 23247: the Physical Twin Entity (hosting sensors, actuators and compute resources of the 1/10‑scale vehicle), the Digital Twin Entity (implementing data management, control logic and off‑loaded ACC algorithms), and the User Entity (providing the 3D visualization and control‑panel UI). Each entity is instantiated on a distinct hardware component and organized as ROS 2 packages containing one or more ROS 2 nodes; no multiple or decentralized twin instances are described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The DTS follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture with an entity‑based decomposition. Software components are mapped to Functional Entities (FEs) and implemented as ROS 2 packages; each package hosts ROS 2 nodes that realize the functionality of its FE. The three services—Data Monitoring, Computation Off‑loading, and Remote Management—are orchestrated through ROS 2 topics and services, enabling modular integration and runtime allocation of compute resources (e.g., cloud or edge) while preserving low‑latency communication with the vehicle. Resource management is achieved by delegating the ACC control loop to the Digital Twin Entity while the Physical Twin Entity handles sensor acquisition and actuation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The DTS incorporates several computational models and data components. The ACC behavioral model, implemented in the Controlling FE of the Digital Twin Entity, receives vehicle state and object track data, identifies the lead vehicle, and computes a desired speed using an analytical control algorithm. A geometric processing model is provided by the Point Cloud LiDAR Clustering & Tracking Data Pre‑Processing FE, which converts raw LiDAR point clouds into tracked object positions. Data sources include a LiDAR sensor (raw point cloud), vehicle state sensors (speed, position, steering angle), and actuator feedback. Data management is realized through a Data Translation FE (ISO 23247 Data Translation) that serialises streams, aligns message types and performs unit conversions; a Data Collection FE gathers incoming streams; a Data Storage FE persists the data; and a Reporting FE generates reports. All software components communicate over a ROS 2 network, while inter‑entity messages are sent over Wi‑Fi UDP. The models are linked in a pipeline: sensor → Data Pre‑Processing FE → ACC Controlling FE → desired‑speed output → Actuation FE.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation in the physical twin is performed by two functional entities: the Motor Actuation FE, which drives the vehicle’s drive‑motor, and the Steering Servo Actuation FE, which commands the steering servo. Both FEs receive control commands (e.g., desired speed, steering angle) from the Digital Twin Entity over the Wi‑Fi UDP link and expose ROS 2 service interfaces for remote execution. The control loop must respect a 100 ms clock‑synchronisation accuracy mandated for safety‑critical vehicular applications, limiting actuation latency. The range of motion and torque limits are not detailed in the documents, but the actuators are described as remote‑controllable via the DT’s Control Panel UI.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The primary sensing hardware is a LiDAR unit that produces raw point‑cloud data, which is consumed by the Point Cloud LiDAR Clustering & Tracking Data Pre‑Processing FE. Additional state sensing includes vehicle‑state measurements (speed, position, motor and steering angles) that feed the Vehicle State FE. Sensor data are packaged into ROS 2 messages and transmitted to the Digital Twin Entity; inter‑entity transfer uses Wi‑Fi UDP. The system requires that all devices share a common notion of time with a maximum deviation of 100 ms, ensuring synchronized data capture. Exact sampling frequencies, resolution, or accuracy specifications are not provided.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is driven by the safety‑critical nature of the ACC use case and the need for low end‑to‑end latency; the architecture enforces a 100 ms clock‑synchronisation bound and uses ROS 2 for deterministic intra‑entity communication. Validation and verification are implicitly handled by the Data Translation FE, which performs unit conversion and message‑type alignment, reducing data‑type errors. Uncertainty sources include sensor noise from the LiDAR and timing jitter; the 100 ms synchronization requirement bounds the timing uncertainty. No explicit quantitative validation procedures or error‑correction mechanisms are described; quality assurance relies on the structured entity‑based design and adherence to ISO 23247 functional entities.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data (LiDAR point cloud, motor status, steering servo position, vehicle state) generated by the Physical Twin Entity is transmitted to the Digital Twin Entity. Raw point‑cloud frames are first processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component, which extracts object tracks. All data streams are passed through the Data Translation functional entity, which serialises the messages, aligns message types and performs unit conversions. Inter‑entity communication between the Physical Twin Entity and the Digital Twin Entity uses a Wi‑Fi network with UDP at the transport layer; intra‑entity software components communicate over a ROS 2 network (DDS). Data are streamed in real time for monitoring; the documents do not state explicit sampling rates, but the workflow is event‑driven by acquisition of new sensor frames. Pre‑processing includes clustering, tracking and unit conversion before integration into the 3D Visualization FE.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Digital Twin Entity sends control commands back to the Physical Twin Entity to actuate the vehicle. Commands include desired speed values computed by the off‑loaded ACC service, ACC enable/disable requests, and emergency‑brake commands issued from the User Interface Control Panel FE. These commands are transmitted over the same Wi‑Fi/UDP link and are processed by the Actuation FE, which forwards them to the vehicle actuators (motor, steering servo). The DT receives confirmation of execution via updated vehicle state data displayed in the 3D Visualization, providing a feedback loop. No explicit validation, authentication, or conflict‑resolution mechanisms are described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The system relies on two complementary communication stacks. Inside each entity, software components are packaged as ROS 2 nodes that exchange messages via the DDS protocol of ROS 2. Between entities (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User), a Wi‑Fi network is used with UDP transport. The architecture requires end‑to‑end latency low enough to support real‑time ACC control; a clock synchronisation accuracy of 100 ms is required for safe operation. No bandwidth figures, redundancy schemes, or security protocols (e.g., TLS) are mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Computation that implements the ACC algorithm is off‑loaded from the resource‑constrained ECU to the Digital Twin, which may run on cloud resources (“allocate compute to better‑suited equipment, e.g., in the cloud”). The DT services are deployed as ROS 2 packages, each representing a functional entity (Data Translation FE, Data Monitoring FE, Controlling FE, etc.). The documents do not give quantitative CPU, memory or storage requirements, nor detail scalability mechanisms or specific cloud/edge platforms. Availability is implied by the need for low latency and continuous monitoring, but no explicit redundancy or reliability provisions are described.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture. Functional Entities (FEs) are identified from a list of software components (Table I) and each FE is implemented as an independent ROS 2 package; a package contains one or more ROS 2 nodes that realise the FE’s functionality. The mapping aligns the User Interface FE with the 3D Visualization and Control Panel (User Entity), data‑management and control mechanisms with the Digital Twin Entity, and data pre‑processing, actuation and device‑communication functions with the Physical Twin Entity. The Physical Twin Entity is introduced as a deviation from the ISO 23247 OME concept to host vehicle‑resident resources such as compute, sensors and actuators. Development was performed by two graduate students starting from a course project. Quality‑assurance is implicit in the ISO‑based structuring and in the possibility of deploying FEs on different hardware components, mirroring the flexibility of automotive standards such as AUTOSAR. The design requires a shared notion of time for computation off‑loading, with a clock‑synchronisation accuracy of 100 ms (though stricter limits are mandated by safety‑critical vehicular standards). OPC Unified Architecture is mentioned as an example protocol that could be used for the Data Collection and Device Control Entity.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system is based on ISO 23247 (parts 1‑4), which defines the overall framework, reference architecture, digital representation of physical manufacturing elements and information exchange. The architecture uses the ISO‑defined entities (Observable Manufacturing Element, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity) and implements them as ROS 2 packages. OPC Unified Architecture is cited as a possible protocol for device coordination. For safety‑critical automotive applications the authors recommend complementing ISO 23247 with ISO 26262 and note that many automotive standards require clock synchronisation tighter than 100 ms. The standard’s independence from specific formats and protocols enables integration with the automotive de‑facto software architecture AUTOSAR, allowing hardware‑agnostic component definition. No specific certification or additional compliance framework beyond referencing these standards is described.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The automotive digital‑twin case study emphasizes that safety‑critical aspects must be treated as a first‑class concern and recommends the adoption of established automotive safety standards, in particular ISO 26262‑1:2018 “Road vehicles – Functional safety”. The authors argue that the ISO 23247 reference architecture (RA) on its own is insufficient for safety‑critical applications and that safety processes defined in ISO 26262 should be integrated into the digital‑twin architecture. They also point out that existing automotive software frameworks such as AUTOSAR, which specify software components in a hardware‑agnostic way, can be reused to embed safety mechanisms in the digital twin. No explicit cybersecurity protocols, access‑control, authentication, or threat‑mitigation strategies are detailed in the provided documents.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

enhancing data processing capabilities,” _Machines_, vol. 13, no. 1, 2025.
DOI [: 10.3390/machines13010019](https://doi.org/10.3390/machines13010019)

[15] X. Liu and I. David, “Ai simulation by digital twins: Systematic survey,
reference framework, and mapping to a standardized architecture,” _Softw_
_Syst Model_, 2025. DOI [: 10.1007/s10270-025-01306-0](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-025-01306-0)

[16] A. Shtofenmakher and G. Shao, “Adaptation of ISO 23247 to aerospace
digital twin applications-on-orbit collision avoidance and space-based
debris detection,” in _AIAA SCITECH 2024 Forum_ . 2024. DOI [: 10.2514/](https://doi.org/10.2514/6.2024-0275)
[6.2024-0275](https://doi.org/10.2514/6.2024-0275)

[17] J. Cederbladh, E. Ferko, and E. Lundin, “Towards adopting a digital
twin framework (iso 23247) for battery systems,” in _ITNG 2024: 21st_

Springer, 2024, pp. 397–404, ISBN : 978-3-031-56599-1.

_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture


1 [https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)
2 [https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU](https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU)

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

V. R EFLECTION AND C ONCLUSIONS


We reported on a case study of an automotive DT, through
which we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247 standard
in engineering such systems. Our experiences highlight both
benefits and limitations of the standard in automotive settings.
On a positive note, we found the standard’s reference
architecture (RA) to be **a useful starting point for auto-**
**motive DTs** as it provides guidance for organizing blocks
of functionality. Additionally, we found the standard **easy to**
**use** as it does not assume substantial DT expertise and has a
comfortable learning curve. Our case was developed by two
graduate students, starting from a course project. [4] Through
this case study, we also highlight the **convenient generality**
of ISO 23247. The independence from specific format and
protocol requirements, as well as the ability to rely on domainspecific technologies in a straightforward manner, facilitated
the transfer of the RA to a new domain (automotive).
On a critical note, rigid adherence to the RA may lead to an
**under-utilization of resources** . In contrast to OMEs, vehicles
are resource-rich systems of their own. Thus, we recommend
promoting the physical twin to a first-class entity in automotive
DT architectures. This will allow for deploying FEs on the
vehicle, potentially in a flexible and dynamic fashion. For
example, our case study deploys the Data Pre-Processing FE
and the Actuation FE onto the physical twin, as opposed to
the DT Entity as prescribed by the ISO 23247 RA. Flexibility
in the deployment of FEs is at the heart of existing automotive
standards—e.g., AUTOSAR, the _de facto_ automotive software
architecture standard—where SW components are defined in a
hardware-agnostic manner, making it easier to retrofit existing
automotive architectures into automotive DT architectures.

Our case study further suggests that the RA is **insufficient for**
**safety-critical applications such as automotive** . Therefore,
we recommend safety to be promoted to a first-class citizen
in automotive DT standards by relying on existing automotive
safety standards—e.g., ISO 26262 [38].
Our work is a first look into the benefits and challenges of
adopting a standardized DT architecture for automotive DTs.
In order to aid the development of proper domain-specific
standards, we call for additional research into the topic, with
a particular emphasis on safety, security, data management,
and considering the regulated nature of the automotive domain, compatibility with existing standards. Additionally, we
encourage the development of automotive DT case studies and
exemplars to provide empirical evidence for further research.
In future work, we plan to research the safety aspects of
automotive DTs in more detail and elicit recommendations

V. R EFLECTION AND C ONCLUSIONS


We reported on a case study of an automotive DT, through
which we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247 standard
in engineering such systems. Our experiences highlight both
benefits and limitations of the standard in automotive settings.
On a positive note, we found the standard’s reference
architecture (RA) to be **a useful starting point for auto-**
**motive DTs** as it provides guidance for organizing blocks
of functionality. Additionally, we found the standard **easy to**
**use** as it does not assume substantial DT expertise and has a
comfortable learning curve. Our case was developed by two
graduate students, starting from a course project. [4] Through
this case study, we also highlight the **convenient generality**
of ISO 23247. The independence from specific format and
protocol requirements, as well as the ability to rely on domainspecific technologies in a straightforward manner, facilitated
the transfer of the RA to a new domain (automotive).
On a critical note, rigid adherence to the RA may lead to an
**under-utilization of resources** . In contrast to OMEs, vehicles
are resource-rich systems of their own. Thus, we recommend
promoting the physical twin to a first-class entity in automotive
DT architectures. This will allow for deploying FEs on the
vehicle, potentially in a flexible and dynamic fashion. For
example, our case study deploys the Data Pre-Processing FE
and the Actuation FE onto the physical twin, as opposed to
the DT Entity as prescribed by the ISO 23247 RA. Flexibility
in the deployment of FEs is at the heart of existing automotive
standards—e.g., AUTOSAR, the _de facto_ automotive software
architecture standard—where SW components are defined in a
hardware-agnostic manner, making it easier to retrofit existing
automotive architectures into automotive DT architectures.

Our case study further suggests that the RA is **insufficient for**
**safety-critical applications such as automotive** . Therefore,
we recommend safety to be promoted to a first-class citizen
in automotive DT standards by relying on existing automotive
safety standards—e.g., ISO 26262 [38].
Our work is a first look into the benefits and challenges of
adopting a standardized DT architecture for automotive DTs.
In order to aid the development of proper domain-specific
standards, we call for additional research into the topic, with
a particular emphasis on safety, security, data management,
and considering the regulated nature of the automotive domain, compatibility with existing standards. Additionally, we
encourage the development of automotive DT case studies and
exemplars to provide empirical evidence for further research.
In future work, we plan to research the safety aspects of
automotive DTs in more detail and elicit recommendations
